title: Check if graph is tree (connected + n-1 edges).
description: Check if graph is tree (connected + n-1 edges).

examples.explanation: Step 1: Count edges = 4. n=5, n-1=4, edges match. Step 2: BFS from node 0. Adjacency: 0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2,4], 4:[3]. Queue: [0]. Visited: {0}. Process 0: neighbors [1], queue: [1], visited: {0,1}. Process 1: neighbors [0,2], queue: [2], visited: {0,1,2}. Process 2: neighbors [1,3], queue: [3], visited: {0,1,2,3}. Process 3: neighbors [2,4], queue: [4], visited: {0,1,2,3,4}. Process 4: neighbors [3], queue: [], visited: {0,1,2,3,4}. Visited count=5=n. Return true.

Step 1: Count edges = 3. n=4, n-1=3, edges match. Step 2: BFS from node 0. Adjacency: 0:[1,2], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,0], 3:[]. Queue: [0]. Visited: {0}. Process 0: neighbors [1,2], queue: [1,2], visited: {0,1,2}. Process 1: neighbors [0,2], queue: [2], visited: {0,1,2}. Process 2: neighbors [1,0], queue: [], visited: {0,1,2}. Visited count=3≠4. Node 3 unreachable. Return false.

approaches.name: Brute Force: DFS/BFS with Edge Count Check
approaches.summary: Perform a DFS or BFS traversal from any node to check if the graph is connected (all nodes reachable). Separately verify that the number of edges equals n-1. This approach directly applies the definition of a tree.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check if Graph is Tree Using BFS/DFS with Edge Count
ladders.desc: Complete solution to determine if an undirected graph is a tree by checking connectivity and edge count.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires verifying if an undirected graph satisfies two properties of a tree: connectivity (all nodes reachable from any starting node) and having exactly n-1 edges for n nodes. The brute force approach uses BFS or DFS to check connectivity and a simple edge count comparison. Intuition: A tree is minimally connected—removing any edge disconnects it—and has exactly n-1 edges, which is the minimum number to connect n nodes without cycles. Step-by-step: 1. Count edges from adjacency list. 2. If edges ≠ n-1, return false (violates tree property). 3. Perform BFS/DFS from node 0 to check if all n nodes are visited. 4. If visited count = n, return true; else false. Time: O(n+m) for traversal and edge counting. Space: O(n) for visited array and queue/stack. Connection to L1: This integrates the connectivity check (L1) with edge validation. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle disconnected graphs with n-1 edges (e.g., two separate components), not using visited array to avoid infinite loops in cycles, or mishandling self-loops (which violate tree properties). Real code pattern: Use adjacency list, BFS queue, and visited set.

examples.trace: Step 1: Count edges = 4. n=5, n-1=4, edges match. Step 2: BFS from node 0. Adjacency: 0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2,4], 4:[3]. Queue: [0]. Visited: {0}. Process 0: neighbors [1], queue: [1], visited: {0,1}. Process 1: neighbors [0,2], queue: [2], visited: {0,1,2}. Process 2: neighbors [1,3], queue: [3], visited: {0,1,2,3}. Process 3: neighbors [2,4], queue: [4], visited: {0,1,2,3,4}. Process 4: neighbors [3], queue: [], visited: {0,1,2,3,4}. Visited count=5=n. Return true.

Step 1: Count edges = 3. n=4, n-1=3, edges match. Step 2: BFS from node 0. Adjacency: 0:[1,2], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,0], 3:[]. Queue: [0]. Visited: {0}. Process 0: neighbors [1,2], queue: [1,2], visited: {0,1,2}. Process 1: neighbors [0,2], queue: [2], visited: {0,1,2}. Process 2: neighbors [1,0], queue: [], visited: {0,1,2}. Visited count=3≠4. Node 3 unreachable. Return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check Graph Connectivity via BFS/DFS
ladders.desc: Determine if all nodes in an undirected graph are reachable from a starting node.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine checks connectivity by traversing the graph from a given node (typically 0) using BFS or DFS and verifying if all n nodes are visited. Intuition: In an undirected graph, if starting from any node we can reach all others, the graph is connected; otherwise, it has disconnected components. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize visited array of size n, all false. 2. Use queue (BFS) or stack (DFS) with starting node, mark visited. 3. While queue/stack not empty: pop node, iterate neighbors, if not visited, mark visited and add to queue/stack. 4. After traversal, count visited nodes; if count = n, graph is connected. Time: O(n+m) where m is edges. Space: O(n) for visited and queue/stack. Connection to L0: This is the core connectivity check in the full problem; combined with edge count, it determines if graph is tree. Common mistakes: Not handling empty graphs (n=0), assuming graph is directed (but problem is undirected), or incorrect neighbor iteration from adjacency list. Real code pattern: Use adjacency list representation and standard BFS/DFS traversal.

examples.trace: Adjacency: 0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2]. BFS from node 0. Queue: [0], visited: [T,F,F,F]. Process 0: neighbors [1], queue: [1], visited: [T,T,F,F]. Process 1: neighbors [0,2], queue: [2], visited: [T,T,T,F]. Process 2: neighbors [1,3], queue: [3], visited: [T,T,T,T]. Process 3: neighbors [2], queue: [], visited: [T,T,T,T]. All 4 nodes visited, return true.

Adjacency: 0:[1], 1:[0], 2:[3], 3:[2,4], 4:[3]. BFS from node 0. Queue: [0], visited: [T,F,F,F,F]. Process 0: neighbors [1], queue: [1], visited: [T,T,F,F,F]. Process 1: neighbors [0], queue: [], visited: [T,T,F,F,F]. Visited nodes: 0,1. Nodes 2,3,4 unreachable. Return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Tree Property: Connectivity + n-1 Edges
ladders.desc: Understand the mathematical definition of a tree in graph theory.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the theoretical insight: an undirected graph is a tree if and only if it is connected and has exactly n-1 edges. Intuition: Connectivity ensures no isolated components, while n-1 edges guarantees no cycles (since a connected graph with n nodes requires at least n-1 edges to be connected, and more edges create cycles). Proof sketch: A tree is defined as a connected acyclic graph. For n nodes, a connected acyclic graph has exactly n-1 edges (proven by induction). Conversely, if a graph is connected and has n-1 edges, it must be acyclic (since adding any edge to a connected graph with n-1 edges creates a cycle). Step-by-step: 1. Verify edges = n-1. 2. Check connectivity via traversal. Time: O(1) for edge check, O(n+m) for connectivity. Space: O(1) for edge count. Connection to L1: This logic justifies why the connectivity check (L1) combined with edge count suffices for the full problem. Common mistakes: Confusing with directed trees, missing that the property holds only for undirected graphs, or not realizing that n-1 edges alone doesn't guarantee connectivity (e.g., two separate components with total n-1 edges). Real code pattern: Simple comparison: if len(edges) != n-1: return False.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check edges = 3, n-1 = 3, matches. Step 2: Check connectivity = true. Both conditions satisfied, so graph is tree.
Step 1: Check edges = 5, n-1 = 4, does not match. Even though connected, extra edge indicates cycle, so not tree.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation as Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Construct an adjacency list from a list of edges for an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves converting a list of edges into an adjacency list, a common graph representation for efficient traversal. Intuition: An adjacency list stores, for each node, a list of its neighboring nodes, making it easy to iterate during BFS/DFS. For undirected graphs, each edge (u,v) adds v to u's list and u to v's list. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize an empty list of size n, each element an empty list. 2. For each edge [u,v] in edges: append v to adj[u] and append u to adj[v]. 3. Return adjacency list. Time: O(m) to process all edges. Space: O(n+m) to store lists. Connection to L2: This representation is needed to check connectivity (part of tree property) efficiently. Common mistakes: Forgetting to add both directions for undirected edges, using incorrect indexing (e.g., 1-based vs 0-based), or not initializing lists properly. Real code pattern: Use list comprehension: adj = [[] for _ in range(n)], then loop through edges.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — BFS Traversal on a Graph
ladders.desc: Perform Breadth-First Search to visit nodes level by level from a start node.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves traversing a graph using BFS, which explores all neighbors at the present depth before moving to nodes at the next depth level. Intuition: BFS uses a queue to ensure nodes are processed in the order they are discovered, making it useful for shortest path problems and connectivity checks. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize visited array (size n, all false). 2. Create queue, enqueue start node, mark visited. 3. While queue not empty: dequeue node, for each neighbor in adjacency list, if not visited, mark visited and enqueue. 4. Continue until queue empty. Time: O(n+m) as each node and edge processed once. Space: O(n) for queue and visited. Connection to L3: BFS requires adjacency list (from L3) to access neighbors efficiently. Common mistakes: Not marking node as visited when enqueuing (causing re-processing), using stack instead of queue (turning into DFS), or incorrect queue operations. Real code pattern: Use collections.deque for queue in Python, with while loop and neighbor iteration.

examples.trace: Visited: [F,F,F,F]. Queue: [0], mark visited[0]=T. Process 0: neighbors [1,2], queue: [1,2], mark visited[1]=T, visited[2]=T. Process 1: neighbors [0,3], queue: [2,3], mark visited[3]=T. Process 2: neighbors [0,3], queue: [3]. Process 3: neighbors [1,2], queue: []. All nodes visited.

Visited: [F,F,F]. Queue: [0], mark visited[0]=T. Process 0: neighbors [1], queue: [1], mark visited[1]=T. Process 1: neighbors [0,2], queue: [2], mark visited[2]=T. Process 2: neighbors [1], queue: []. All nodes visited.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Basics: Nodes, Edges, and Connectivity
ladders.desc: Understand fundamental graph concepts: nodes, edges, and what it means for a graph to be connected.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level introduces basic graph theory concepts for beginners. A graph consists of nodes (vertices) and edges connecting pairs of nodes. In an undirected graph, edges have no direction. Connectivity means that for any two nodes, there is a path between them; if not, the graph is disconnected with multiple components. Intuition: Think of nodes as cities and edges as roads; if you can travel from any city to any other via roads, the map is connected. Step-by-step: 1. Define graph with n nodes. 2. List edges as pairs (u,v). 3. To check connectivity, ensure no node is isolated from others. Time: O(1) for conceptual understanding. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This concept underpins why BFS (L4) can check connectivity by traversing paths. Common mistakes: Confusing directed and undirected edges, misunderstanding that connectivity requires paths, not direct edges. Real code pattern: Represent nodes as integers 0 to n-1, edges as list of lists.

examples.trace: Nodes: 0,1,2. Edges: 0-1 and 1-2. Path from 0 to 2: 0→1→2. All nodes reachable, so connected.
Nodes: 0,1,2,3. Edges: 0-1 and 2-3. No path between nodes in {0,1} and {2,3}. Two components, so disconnected.

approaches.name: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) with Cycle Detection
approaches.summary: Use union-find to process edges: if adding an edge connects two nodes already in the same set, a cycle is detected, so it's not a tree. After processing all edges, check if exactly n-1 edges were added and the graph is connected (one root).

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check if Graph is Tree Using Union-Find
ladders.desc: Complete solution using union-find to detect cycles and check connectivity for tree validation.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires checking if an undirected graph with n nodes and m edges is a tree. A tree must satisfy two conditions: (1) It must be connected (all nodes reachable from each other), and (2) It must have exactly n-1 edges (no cycles). The union-find approach elegantly handles both conditions simultaneously. The intuition is that as we process edges, we're building connected components. If we ever try to union two nodes that are already in the same component, we've found a cycle, which immediately disqualifies the graph from being a tree. After processing all edges, we need to verify that we have exactly n-1 edges (which we track during processing) and that all nodes belong to the same component (connected). The union-find data structure with path compression and union by rank gives us near-constant time operations, making this approach efficient. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize union-find with n nodes. 2. For each edge (u, v): a. If find(u) == find(v), return False (cycle detected). b. Otherwise, union(u, v) and increment edge count. 3. After processing all edges, check if edge count == n-1 and all nodes have the same root (connected). Time complexity is O(n + m * α(n)) where α is the inverse Ackermann function (effectively constant). Space is O(n) for parent and rank arrays. Common mistakes include forgetting to check both conditions (cycle detection AND connectivity), not handling the edge count properly, or implementing union-find incorrectly. The connection to L1 is that this level uses the complete union-find implementation as a black box to solve the full problem.

examples.trace: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3,4], rank=[0,0,0,0,0], edgeCount=0. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, different, union(0,1): parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1, edgeCount=1. Edge [0,2]: find(0)=0, find(2)=2, different, union(0,2): parent[2]=0, rank[0]=1, edgeCount=2. Edge [0,3]: find(0)=0, find(3)=3, different, union(0,3): parent[3]=0, rank[0]=1, edgeCount=3. Edge [1,4]: find(1)=0 (after path compression), find(4)=4, different, union(0,4): parent[4]=0, rank[0]=1, edgeCount=4. After all edges: edgeCount=4, n-1=4, check connectivity: all nodes have root 0. Return True.

Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3], rank=[0,0,0,0], edgeCount=0. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, different, union(0,1): parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1, edgeCount=1. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=0, find(2)=2, different, union(0,2): parent[2]=0, rank[0]=1, edgeCount=2. Edge [2,0]: find(2)=0, find(0)=0, same root! Cycle detected. Return False immediately.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Union-Find Implementation with Path Compression and Union by Rank
ladders.desc: Complete union-find data structure implementation for efficient connectivity queries.
ladders.explanation: Union-find (disjoint set union) is a data structure that maintains a collection of disjoint sets and supports two key operations: find (determine which set an element belongs to) and union (merge two sets). For tree checking, we need to efficiently determine if two nodes are connected and merge components when adding edges. The intuition behind optimization is to keep trees flat (path compression) and balanced (union by rank). Path compression makes find operations faster by updating parent pointers directly to the root during traversal. Union by rank ensures we attach smaller trees under larger ones to minimize height. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize parent array where each node is its own parent, and rank array with zeros. 2. find(x): if parent[x] != x, recursively set parent[x] = find(parent[x]) and return parent[x]. 3. union(x, y): find roots rx and ry. If rx == ry, return False (already connected). Else, if rank[rx] < rank[ry], parent[rx] = ry. Else if rank[rx] > rank[ry], parent[ry] = rx. Else (equal ranks), parent[ry] = rx and increment rank[rx]. Return True. Time complexity per operation is O(α(n)) amortized. Space is O(n). Common mistakes include not implementing path compression correctly (forgetting to update parent pointers), confusing rank with size, or not handling the return values properly. This feeds into L0 by providing the core data structure that the full solution uses to track connectivity and detect cycles.

examples.input: Initialize with 4 nodes, operations: union(0,1), union(1,2), find(0), find(2)
examples.trace: Start: parent=[0,1,2,3], rank=[0,0,0,0]. union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, rank[0]=0, rank[1]=0, equal, parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. union(1,2): find(1)=0 (after path compression), find(2)=2, rank[0]=1, rank[2]=0, parent[2]=0, rank[0]=1. find(0): parent[0]=0, return 0. find(2): parent[2]=0, recursively find(0)=0, set parent[2]=0, return 0.

examples.input: Initialize with 5 nodes, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(1,3), find(4)
examples.trace: Start: parent=[0,1,2,3,4], rank=[0,0,0,0,0]. union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, equal ranks, parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. union(2,3): find(2)=2, find(3)=3, equal ranks, parent[3]=2, rank[2]=1. union(1,3): find(1)=0, find(3)=2, rank[0]=1, rank[2]=1, equal, parent[2]=0, rank[0]=2. find(4): parent[4]=4, return 4.

testCases.input: n=3, operations: union(0,1), union(1,2), find(0), find(2)
n=4, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), find(0), find(3)
n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(1,3), find(0), find(2)
n=6, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(1,3), union(3,5), find(0), find(4)
n=4, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(0,2), find(3)
n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(1,2), union(2,3), union(3,4), find(4)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Cycle Detection During Edge Processing
ladders.desc: Using union-find to detect cycles while building the graph.
ladders.explanation: The central insight for tree checking is that a tree has no cycles, and we can detect cycles efficiently as we add edges using union-find. The intuition is simple: if we try to add an edge between two nodes that are already connected (i.e., in the same union-find component), then adding this edge would create a cycle. This works because union-find maintains connectivity information incrementally. As we process each edge (u, v), we check if find(u) == find(v). If they are equal, we've found a cycle and can immediately conclude the graph is not a tree. If they are different, we perform union(u, v) to merge their components and continue. This process alone doesn't guarantee a tree—we still need to check connectivity and edge count—but it efficiently rules out graphs with cycles. The step-by-step algorithm for this sub-problem: 1. Initialize union-find for n nodes. 2. For each edge (u, v) in the edge list: a. If find(u) == find(v), return False (cycle detected). b. Else, union(u, v). 3. If no cycle detected, return True. Time complexity is O(m * α(n)) for processing m edges. Space is O(n). Common mistakes include not checking for cycles at the right time (e.g., after all unions) or misunderstanding that cycle detection alone is insufficient for tree validation. This feeds into L1 by being the primary application of the union-find operations, where the cycle check relies on the find operation.

examples.trace: Initialize union-find. Edge [0,1]: find(0)≠find(1), union. Edge [1,2]: find(1)≠find(2), union. Edge [2,3]: find(2)≠find(3), union. No cycle detected.
Initialize union-find. Edge [0,1]: find(0)≠find(1), union. Edge [1,2]: find(1)≠find(2), union. Edge [2,0]: find(2)==find(0) (both in same component), cycle detected, return False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Connectivity Check in Union-Find
ladders.desc: Determining if all nodes are connected by checking for a single root.
ladders.explanation: After processing edges without cycles, we need to verify the graph is connected. In union-find terms, connectivity means all nodes belong to the same set (i.e., have the same root). The intuition is that if we've merged components correctly during edge processing, the final structure should have exactly one root representing the entire connected component. To check this, we iterate through all nodes, compute their roots using find, and ensure all roots are identical. Alternatively, we can count distinct roots—if count > 1, the graph is disconnected. This check is crucial because a graph with n-1 edges and no cycles could still be disconnected (e.g., two separate trees). Step-by-step: 1. After processing edges, initialize a variable root = find(0). 2. For each node i from 1 to n-1: if find(i) != root, return False. 3. If all match, return True. Time complexity is O(n * α(n)) for n find operations. Space is O(1) extra. Common mistakes include not using find (using parent directly, which may not be the root due to path compression) or forgetting to check connectivity after cycle detection. This feeds into L2 by adding the connectivity verification to the cycle detection logic, completing the tree validation.

examples.trace: root = find(0) = 0. Check node 1: find(1)=0, matches. Node 2: find(2)=0, matches. Node 3: find(3)=0, matches. All match, connected.
root = find(0)=0. Check node 1: find(1)=0, matches. Node 2: find(2)=2, does not match root 0. Return False, disconnected.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Find with Path Compression
ladders.desc: Recursive find operation that compresses paths to optimize future queries.
ladders.explanation: The find operation is fundamental to union-find: it returns the root (representative) of the set containing a given element. Path compression is an optimization that flattens the tree structure during find operations, making future queries faster. The intuition is that after finding the root for a node, we update its parent pointer directly to the root, so subsequent finds for this node or its children will be O(1). This is achieved recursively: if a node is not the root (parent[x] != x), we recursively find the root of its parent and set parent[x] to that root before returning. Step-by-step: function find(x): if parent[x] != x: parent[x] = find(parent[x]). return parent[x]. This ensures that after a find, the node and all ancestors point directly to the root. Time complexity is O(α(n)) amortized due to path compression. Space is O(n) for recursion stack in worst case (can be iterative to avoid this). Common mistakes include not updating the parent pointer (missing the assignment) or implementing it iteratively without proper compression. This feeds into L3 as the tool used to check connectivity by comparing roots of different nodes.

examples.trace: find(0): parent[0]=1≠0, so find(1). find(1): parent[1]=2≠1, so find(2). find(2): parent[2]=2, return 2. Back to find(1): set parent[1]=2, return 2. Back to find(0): set parent[0]=2, return 2.

find(3): parent[3]=2≠3, so find(2). find(2): parent[2]=1≠2, so find(1). find(1): parent[1]=0≠1, so find(0). find(0): parent[0]=0, return 0. Back to find(1): set parent[1]=0, return 0. Back to find(2): set parent[2]=0, return 0. Back to find(3): set parent[3]=0, return 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Parent Array Representation of Sets
ladders.desc: Representing disjoint sets using an array where each element points to its parent.
ladders.explanation: The foundation of union-find is representing sets as trees stored in an array. Each index in the array corresponds to a node, and the value at that index is the parent of that node. If a node is its own parent (parent[i] == i), it is the root of its set. This simple structure allows us to model connectivity: nodes in the same tree belong to the same set. The intuition is that we can follow parent pointers from any node up to the root to determine its set membership. Initially, with n nodes, we set parent[i] = i for all i, meaning each node is in its own singleton set. This representation is efficient for union and find operations. Step-by-step initialization: parent = list(range(n)). To find the root of a node x, we repeatedly follow parent pointers until we reach a node where parent[x] == x. This naive find has O(h) time where h is tree height. Union merges two trees by making one root point to the other. This basic version without optimizations is understandable for beginners. Time complexity per operation can be O(n) in worst case without optimizations. Space is O(n). Common mistakes include confusing parent pointers or not initializing correctly. This feeds into L4 as the underlying data structure that the optimized find operation improves upon.

examples.trace: For n=3, create array of indices 0,1,2. Set parent[0]=0, parent[1]=1, parent[2]=2. Each node is its own root.

examples.input: n=4, perform union(0,1) by setting parent[1]=0
examples.trace: Start: parent=[0,1,2,3]. To union(0,1), find root of 0 is 0, root of 1 is 1. Make root 1 point to root 0: set parent[1]=0. Parent array becomes [0,0,2,3]. Now nodes 0 and 1 are in same set.

testCases.input: n=3, initial parent, find root of 0
n=2, parent=[0,0], are nodes 0 and 1 connected?
n=3, parent=[0,1,2], are nodes 0 and 1 connected?

approaches.name: Adjacency Matrix with Properties Check
approaches.summary: Construct an adjacency matrix from the graph and use matrix properties (e.g., checking the rank or eigenvalues) to verify connectivity and edge count. This relies on linear algebra concepts rather than graph algorithms.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check if Graph is Tree Using Adjacency Matrix Properties
ladders.desc: Complete solution using adjacency matrix and linear algebra to verify connectivity and edge count.
ladders.explanation: This approach leverages linear algebra properties of the adjacency matrix to determine if a graph is a tree. A tree with n vertices must have exactly n-1 edges and be connected. We construct the adjacency matrix A from the graph, then compute the Laplacian matrix L = D - A, where D is the degree matrix (diagonal matrix with vertex degrees). For a connected graph, the Laplacian matrix has rank n-1. We verify two conditions: 1) The graph has exactly n-1 edges (count edges from A), and 2) The Laplacian matrix has rank n-1 (indicating connectivity). The rank is computed via Gaussian elimination to find the number of non-zero rows in row echelon form. Intuition: The Laplacian matrix's rank relates to the number of connected components; for a connected graph, it's n-1. Steps: 1) Build adjacency matrix A from input edges. 2) Count edges: sum of upper triangle of A (undirected graph). 3) Build degree matrix D from A. 4) Compute L = D - A. 5) Perform Gaussian elimination on L to compute rank. 6) Check if edge count == n-1 and rank == n-1. Time: O(n^3) due to Gaussian elimination. Space: O(n^2) for matrices. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into the final solution. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle undirected graphs (symmetric A), incorrect edge counting (divide by 2 for undirected), or miscomputing rank (off-by-one errors). Real code pattern: Use nested loops for matrix operations and Gaussian elimination with partial pivoting for stability.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Rank of Laplacian Matrix via Gaussian Elimination
ladders.desc: Perform Gaussian elimination on the Laplacian matrix to determine its rank.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine computes the rank of a square matrix (specifically the Laplacian matrix L) using Gaussian elimination, which transforms the matrix into row echelon form (REF). The rank is the number of non-zero rows in REF. Intuition: Gaussian elimination reduces the matrix to a form where linear dependencies are revealed; zero rows indicate linear dependence, so the count of non-zero rows gives the dimension of the row space (rank). Steps: 1) Create a copy of matrix L. 2) For each column i from 0 to n-1: a) Find pivot row (row with non-zero element in column i, starting from row i). b) If no pivot, continue to next column. c) Swap pivot row with current row i. d) Normalize pivot row (divide by pivot element). e) Eliminate column i from all rows below and above (for full reduction, but for rank, below is sufficient). 3) Count non-zero rows in resulting matrix. Time: O(n^3) due to triple nested loops. Space: O(n^2) for matrix copy. Connection: This rank computation is used in L0 to check connectivity (rank == n-1). Common mistakes: Not handling zero pivots correctly (leads to division by zero), forgetting to swap rows, or incorrect elimination steps. Real code pattern: Use loops with careful indexing and floating-point tolerances for near-zero values.

examples.trace: Step 1: Copy matrix. Step 2: Column 0, pivot row 0 (element 1). Swap row0 with itself. Normalize: row0 = [1,-1,0]. Eliminate: row1 += row0 → [0,1,-1]. Row2 unchanged [0,-1,1]. Step 3: Column 1, pivot row 1 (element 1). Normalize: row1 = [0,1,-1]. Eliminate: row2 += row1 → [0,0,0]. Step 4: Column 2, no pivot (all zeros in column 2 from row2 onward). Step 5: Result: [[1,-1,0],[0,1,-1],[0,0,0]]. Non-zero rows: 2 → rank=2.

Step 1: Copy matrix. Step 2: Column 0, pivot row 0 (element 2). Normalize: row0 = [1,-0.5,-0.5]. Eliminate: row1 += row0 → [0,1.5,-1.5]. Row2 += row0 → [0,-1.5,1.5]. Step 3: Column 1, pivot row 1 (element 1.5). Normalize: row1 = [0,1,-1]. Eliminate: row2 += row1 → [0,0,0]. Step 4: Column 2, no pivot. Step 5: Result: [[1,-0.5,-0.5],[0,1,-1],[0,0,0]]. Non-zero rows: 2 → rank=2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Construct Laplacian Matrix from Adjacency Matrix
ladders.desc: Build the Laplacian matrix L = D - A from the adjacency matrix A and degree matrix D.
ladders.explanation: This core logic constructs the Laplacian matrix, a key linear algebra tool for graph analysis. Given an adjacency matrix A (n x n) for an undirected graph, we first compute the degree matrix D, which is a diagonal matrix where D[i][i] = degree of vertex i (sum of row i in A, since A is symmetric). Then, the Laplacian matrix L is computed as L = D - A. Intuition: The Laplacian matrix encodes the graph structure; its properties (like rank) reveal connectivity. For a tree, L has rank n-1. Steps: 1) Initialize D as zero matrix of size n x n. 2) For each vertex i, compute degree = sum(A[i][j] for j in 0..n-1). 3) Set D[i][i] = degree. 4) Compute L[i][j] = D[i][j] - A[i][j] for all i,j. Time: O(n^2) to compute degrees and subtract matrices. Space: O(n^2) for D and L. Connection: This Laplacian matrix is used in L1 for rank computation. Common mistakes: Incorrect degree calculation (e.g., not summing entire row), mishandling directed graphs (A should be symmetric for undirected), or creating D as non-diagonal. Real code pattern: Use nested loops to compute sums and matrix subtraction.

ladders.title: Building Block — Build Adjacency Matrix from Edge List
ladders.desc: Convert a list of edges into an adjacency matrix representation of the graph.
ladders.explanation: This building block creates an adjacency matrix A of size n x n from an input list of edges. For an undirected graph, each edge (u,v) is represented by setting A[u][v] = 1 and A[v][u] = 1. The matrix is initialized with zeros. Intuition: The adjacency matrix provides a compact way to represent graph connectivity, enabling linear algebra operations. Steps: 1) Initialize an n x n matrix with all zeros. 2) For each edge (u,v) in the edge list: a) Validate u and v are within 0..n-1. b) Set A[u][v] = 1 and A[v][u] = 1 (for undirected). 3) Return A. Time: O(n^2) to initialize matrix + O(e) to fill edges, where e is number of edges. Space: O(n^2) for the matrix. Connection: This matrix is used in L2 to compute the Laplacian. Common mistakes: Not handling duplicate edges (should set to 1 regardless), incorrect indexing (0-based vs 1-based), or forgetting to set both directions for undirected graphs. Real code pattern: Use list comprehension for initialization and a loop over edges.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Count Edges from Adjacency Matrix
ladders.desc: Calculate the number of edges in an undirected graph given its adjacency matrix.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation computes the total number of edges in an undirected graph represented by an adjacency matrix A. Since A is symmetric (A[i][j] = A[j][i]), each edge is counted twice if we sum all entries. To avoid double-counting, we sum only the upper triangle (including diagonal for self-loops, but trees have no self-loops). For an undirected graph with no self-loops, the number of edges = (sum of all entries) / 2. Alternatively, sum A[i][j] for i < j. Intuition: Each edge contributes 1 to two cells in A, so dividing by 2 gives the correct count. Steps: 1) Initialize edge_count = 0. 2) For i from 0 to n-1: For j from i+1 to n-1: edge_count += A[i][j]. 3) Return edge_count. Time: O(n^2) to iterate over matrix. Space: O(1). Connection: This edge count is used in L0 to check if edges == n-1. Common mistakes: Double-counting by summing entire matrix without dividing, including self-loops in count, or using wrong loop bounds. Real code pattern: Use nested loops with inner loop starting at i+1.

examples.trace: Step 1: edge_count=0. Step 2: i=0: j=1: A[0][1]=1 → edge_count=1; j=2: A[0][2]=0 → no change. i=1: j=2: A[1][2]=1 → edge_count=2. i=2: no j (j starts at 3). Result: edge_count=2. Verify: Edges are (0,1) and (1,2).
Step 1: edge_count=0. Step 2: i=0: j=1: A[0][1]=1 → edge_count=1; j=2: A[0][2]=1 → edge_count=2. i=1: j=2: A[1][2]=1 → edge_count=3. Result: edge_count=3. Verify: Edges are (0,1), (0,2), (1,2).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Matrix Representation and Basic Operations
ladders.desc: Understand matrices, initialization, and element access in programming.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the basic concept of matrices as two-dimensional arrays in programming, used to represent data like graph adjacency. A matrix is a grid of numbers with rows and columns. In Python, it can be represented as a list of lists, where each inner list is a row. Key operations: 1) Initialization: Create an n x n matrix with all zeros using nested loops or list comprehension. 2) Element access: Access or modify the element at row i and column j using matrix[i][j]. 3) Iteration: Use nested loops to traverse all elements. Intuition: Matrices provide a structured way to store pairwise relationships, such as graph edges. Steps for initialization: matrix = [[0 for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(n)]. Time: O(n^2) to create. Space: O(n^2). Connection: This is used in L4 to build and manipulate adjacency matrices. Common mistakes: Incorrect indexing (off-by-one errors), shallow copying issues (using * operator for lists), or misunderstanding row vs column order. Real code pattern: Use list comprehension for clarity and efficiency.

examples.trace: Step 1: Use list comprehension: matrix = [[0 for _ in range(2)] for _ in range(2)]. Step 2: Outer loop creates 2 rows. Step 3: Inner loop creates 2 columns per row. Result: Row 0: [0,0], Row 1: [0,0]. Full matrix: [[0,0],[0,0]].

Step 1: Initialize 3x3 zeros: [[0,0,0],[0,0,0],[0,0,0]]. Step 2: Access matrix[1][2] (row 1, column 2, 0-based). Step 3: Set matrix[1][2] = 5. Result: Row 0: [0,0,0], Row 1: [0,0,5], Row 2: [0,0,0]. Full matrix: [[0,0,0],[0,0,5],[0,0,0]].
